feed.title: Re: March 23rd 2021 Taproot Activation Meeting Notes

entry.title: Re: March 23rd 2021 Taproot Activation Meeting Notes
entry.summary:
Rusty Russell articulates a perspective on Bitcoin's strategic approach towards risk and conflict resolution, emphasizing the value of deescalation over brinkmanship. He argues that having the option to deescalate strengthens Bitcoin by reducing unnecessary risks. This viewpoint challenges the necessity of adopting a brinkmanship strategy, especially when other options exist that could potentially avoid high-stake confrontations.
Russell highlights concerns with the LOT=true proposal, pointing out its significant drawbacks, such as the need to disregard blocks from miners who might not be actively participating (referred to as "apathetic miners") and the potential for causing a chain split if the economic majority's support is incorrectly assessed due to the lack of intrinsic tooling. These issues underline the complexity and risks associated with implementing this approach without careful consideration and adequate tools.
Despite recognizing the utility of brinkmanship in certain scenarios, Russell suggests that normalizing such a strategy could be detrimental in the long run. He advocates for the development of specialized tooling to manage situations requiring brinkmanship, well ahead of any crisis. Moreover, he notes that it is possible to prepare a User-Activated Soft Fork (UASF) branch in conjunction with Speedy Trial (ST), even without needing to set the LockinOnTimeout (LOT) parameter to false. This approach, however, would introduce more complexity into the code and require adjustments to the timing of specific actions.
In conclusion, while acknowledging the counterargument presented in the form of a Negative Acknowledgement (NACK), Russell expresses his willingness to support and run code that diverges from this viewpoint, based on his analysis of the strategic implications and technical considerations involved.
